ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general french fries entry; curly/spiral variant not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried potato products broadly; no specific curly fries sub-code exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for curly fries; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One large serving of curly fries is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a standard fast-food large portion as listed on the GrabFood platform.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Potatoes, french fried, all types, salt added in processing, frozen, oven-heated (FDC ID 170697) and Arby's Curly Fries (FDC ID 2103689)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central branded entry for seasoned curly fries (Arby's Curly Fries, FDC ID 2103689) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried potato products. Input per_100g values (energy 300 kcal, protein 3.0 g, fat 17.5 g, carbohydrate 32.5 g, sodium 400 mg) are consistent with USDA branded curly fries data and have been retained. Micronutrients (calcium 10 mg, iron 0.75 mg, vitamin C 5.0 mg) cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried potato data. Cholesterol confirmed at 0 mg for plant-based fried product. Trans fat 0.25 g and saturated fat 3.0 g retained from input, consistent with USDA branded data range.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — French fries / fried potato products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for seasoned curly fries in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on French fries (GI ~75, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008, Table entry for french fries). Deep-frying and wheat flour coating are consistent with high-GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (75 × 65.0) / 100 = 48.75, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 32.5 × 2 = 65.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100 g (800 mg per large serving) from seasoning blend
hypertension.guidance: A single large serving delivers 800 mg sodium — approximately 35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — making this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension; request unseasoned preparation and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as ketchup or mayonnaise.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) and 65 g available carbohydrate per large serving producing a glycemic load of ~49
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of deep-frying, wheat flour coating, and 32.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g yields a high-GI, high-GL food that causes significant postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit portion size and pair with low-GI foods if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (17.5 g/100 g), saturated fat (3.0 g/100 g), trans fat (0.25 g/100 g), and high sodium (400 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-fried curly fries contribute significant saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium per serving — all established cardiovascular risk factors — and should be avoided or consumed only occasionally by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); potato is a nightshade vegetable
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation is a hard disqualifier for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation that worsens joint inflammation; potatoes as a nightshade may also trigger solanine-related flares in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curly Fries (Large) are spiral-cut, skin-on potato fries that are deep-fried in vegetable oil and coated with a seasoned spice blend typically containing salt, onion powder, garlic powder, and paprika. The curly shape is achieved by cutting potatoes with a spiral blade, and the wheat flour coating creates a crispy, golden exterior with a soft, fluffy interior. Per 100 g, they provide approximately 300 kcal, 17.5 g fat (including 3.0 g saturated fat and 0.25 g trans fat), 32.5 g carbohydrate, 3.0 g dietary fibre, 3.0 g protein, and 400 mg sodium. A standard large serving is approximately 200 g, delivering 600 kcal, 65 g carbohydrate, and 800 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 75 (high), producing a glycemic load of ~49 per large serving — clinically significant for blood glucose management. This food is classified as ultra-processed due to the seasoning blend and deep-frying method. It is suitable for vegetarians and halal diets but contains gluten (wheat flour coating). Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high GI and GL), cardiovascular disease (trans fat, saturated fat, sodium), or rheumatoid arthritis (AGE formation from deep-frying, nightshade ingredient). In Singapore, curly fries are commonly served as a side dish at Western fast-food outlets and casual dining restaurants.
common_user_queries: How many calories in large curly fries?
Are curly fries suitable for diabetics?
Curly fries vs regular fries nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central branded curly fries data and have been retained from the input. Micronutrient values are cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried potato data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database french fries entry. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact curly fries entry exists in a primary regional source (HPB Singapore or USDA exact match); values are adapted from the closest approved branded and SR Legacy sources.
notes: G3 SENTINEL DOCUMENTATION: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are all set to -1 because detailed fatty acid breakdown (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), full B-vitamin panel (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E), minerals (potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), and moisture data for seasoned curly fries are not consistently reported in USDA FoodData Central branded entries (FDC ID 2103689) or USDA SR Legacy fried potato products as of 2026-04-16; these fields are confirmed unavailable in the consulted approved sources. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — confirmed zero for a plant-based deep-fried potato product with no animal-derived ingredients; this is naturally zero as potatoes and vegetable oil contain no cholesterol. Input ethnic_tags contained 'Wings' and 'Fried Chicken' which are clearly erroneous for a potato fries product; these have been removed. Country of origin corrected to United States (origin of curly fries concept) served in Singapore context. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on french fries data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct curly fries GI entry exists in any approved source.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Curly Fries (Large)' is precise and unambiguous. Mandatory multilingual names present: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi — satisfying G6 for a Singapore-served Western fast-food item.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 300.0, protein_g 3.0, fat_g 17.5, carbohydrate_g 32.5 all present and numeric. Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central branded curly fries (FDC ID 2103689) and SR Legacy fried potato data. nutrient_source fully documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 3.0, sodium_mg 400.0, saturated_fat_g 3.0, sugar_g 0.5 — all present as numeric values, plausible and consistent with USDA data.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 75 (high), gi_category 'high', glycemic_load_per_serving 48.75, gi_source fully documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database french fries entry — approved GI source. Estimation basis clearly documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03302050, french fries food product) with 'closest_parent' match type and gap note. FoodEx2 A00LC (fried potato products) provided with 'closest_parent' match type and gap note. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correct), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.
